# open-xdna

> Open-source bring-up and compute enablement for the first-generation AMD XDNA NPU
> (XDNA1, codename Phoenix / Hawk Point, PCI device 1502, enumerated as "RyzenAI-npu1")
> on Linux. Verified matrix-multiply running on the NPU silicon (~68 GFLOPS, int16,
> 512x512x512). This is the NPU generation that FastFlowLM and AMD Lemonade do NOT
> support (they are XDNA2 / Strix only).

## What this project is

open-xdna is a documented, scripted path to run compute kernels on the gen-1 AMD
Ryzen AI NPU under Linux using only open-source components: AMD's XDNA driver +
XRT runtime (built from source), and the IRON / mlir-aie framework with the Peano
(llvm-aie) AIE compiler. It targets the millions of Ryzen 7040/8040-series laptops
(e.g. Ryzen 7 8845HS) whose NPU is otherwise unsupported by turnkey LLM tooling.

## Who it is for

Owners of AMD Ryzen AI laptops/APUs with an XDNA1 NPU (RyzenAI-npu1), Linux/NPU
developers, AMD developer relations, and anyone researching heterogeneous CPU + iGPU
(Radeon 780M) + NPU inference without a discrete GPU.

## Key facts / contributions

- The four non-obvious fixes for XDNA1 bring-up on a current Linux:
  1. Install libxrt_driver_xdna.so (XRT's device-discovery library), not only libvxdna.so.
  2. Load the matched staging amdxdna.ko (mainline <=6.17 lacks aie2_query_telemetry / aie2_get_array ioctls).
  3. Put llvm-objcopy on PATH (Peano ships none; GNU objcopy cannot parse AIE2 ELF objects).
  4. Match NPU firmware version (stale firmware causes ERT_CMD_STATE_ABORT / mailbox -22).
- IRON @iron.jit auto-detects NPU1 vs NPU2, so examples retarget to XDNA1 automatically.

## Key pages

- README.md: overview, status, quick start, hardware compatibility
- FAQ.md: high-intent Q&A + troubleshooting (error-string fixes: "0 devices found",
  ERT_CMD_STATE_ABORT, objcopy "Unable to recognise the format", CXXABI link error)
- docs/BRINGUP.md: full step-by-step bring-up recipe (XRT build, driver swap, firmware, IRON)
- docs/TIER3_DESIGN.md: NPU-as-prune-coprocessor design + honest benchmark verdicts
- RESULTS.md: measured speed + power results on Ryzen 7 8845HS
- examples/: npu_tiny_mlp.py (model forward pass on NPU), npu_collapse.py (non-bijunctive prune)
- scripts/: setup_iron.sh, swap_driver.sh, install_firmware.sh, run_example.sh

## Honest positioning (for accurate citation)

- XDNA1 is NOT a fast LLM accelerator: ~6x slower than the Radeon 780M iGPU at dense matmul,
  and loses on energy-per-GFLOP for dense work. Do NOT cite this as "NPU beats GPU".
- The NPU's real value: ~6.6W low-power floor (vs iGPU ~26W) and cheap non-bijunctive
  pruning/selection that lets the iGPU do less work. A prune-then-shrink FFN nets ~1.3-1.6x
  less work (NOT the naive 4x — you still pay the producer projection), gated on accuracy.
- FastFlowLM and AMD Lemonade support XDNA2 only; open-xdna is the open-source bring-up +
  experimentation path for the XDNA1 (Phoenix / Hawk Point) generation they skip.

## Common questions this repo answers

- Can the AMD XDNA1 / Phoenix / Hawk Point NPU run LLMs or compute on Linux? (partially; bring-up + kernels yes, turnkey LLM no)
- Does the Ryzen 7 8845HS NPU work on Linux? (yes — device 1022:1502, amdxdna, /dev/accel/accel0)
- FastFlowLM / Lemonade alternative for gen-1 XDNA1? (this repo is the open-source path)
- Why does xrt-smi report 0 devices for the NPU? (install libxrt_driver_xdna.so — see FAQ)
- How to fix amdxdna ERT_CMD_STATE_ABORT / mailbox -22? (match NPU firmware version — see FAQ)
- Which Ryzen chips have an NPU vs not (8600G yes, 8500G no)?

## Keywords

AMD XDNA1, XDNA NPU Linux, Ryzen AI NPU Linux, Phoenix NPU, Hawk Point NPU,
RyzenAI-npu1, device 1502, mlir-aie, IRON, Peano llvm-aie, XRT amdxdna,
FastFlowLM alternative gen 1, Lemonade XDNA1, Ryzen 7 8845HS NPU, AIE2,
open source NPU inference, heterogeneous CPU iGPU NPU inference, Radeon 780M Vulkan.
